content.text: What Are Tools in Agent Systems?

An LLM can only produce text. Tools extend an agent's capabilities beyond text generation by giving it the ability to act on the world: search the web, query a database, call an API, read a file, execute code. Without tools, an agent is limited to what it can derive from its training data. With tools, it can fetch fresh information, compute exact results, and cause side effects.
From the model's perspective, a tool is a
callable function with a known signature

. The model receives a description of available tools, decides which to call, emits a structured request (name + arguments), and the host application executes it and returns the result. The model never executes code directly — it only requests execution.
Every tool definition passed to the Anthropic API has three parts: a
. The schema is a JSON Schema object that constrains what arguments the model can pass.
Key design points in the tool above:
: the model reads the description to decide whether to call this tool at all. A vague description like 'get weather' causes the model to use the tool in the wrong contexts. Specify
prevents the model from inventing values like 'kelvin' or 'C'.

. Optional arguments should have defaults described in their
Signal completion or request human input
Structural — end or pause the loop

The distinction between read and write tools matters for safety and caching. Read tools can be retried freely and cached. Write tools must be idempotent (or guarded) because a failed retry could execute them twice, and they should never be cached.
After dispatching a tool call, the application appends the result as a
message and calls the API again. The model reads the result and either calls another tool or produces a final text response.
Two patterns are essential in the tool dispatch loop: always match
messages (the model uses the ID to correlate results with requests), and always append the assistant's
response block list (not just the text) — the model needs to see its own tool call in the history.
When multiple tools are available, the model selects which one(s) to call based on their descriptions and the current context. For independent sub-tasks, the model may emit
multiple tool calls in a single response
, allowing the host to execute them in parallel.

The quality of the tool definitions directly determines whether the model selects the right tool and constructs valid arguments. Poor descriptions cause tool misuse; ambiguous schemas cause argument errors.
tools. The model cannot partially apply a compound tool.
Write descriptions for the model, not the human
: the description is a prompt, not a docstring. It should say when to call this tool and what it does, not how it is implemented.
: every required field the model must fill is a chance for hallucination. Optional fields with sensible defaults keep the schema simpler.
. The schema enforces it; the description merely suggests.
: free text tool results are harder for the model to parse than

Tools fail. The network is down, the API rate-limits, the record is not found. A robust agentic loop must handle tool errors gracefully — retrying transient failures, reporting permanent failures clearly, and never passing raw exception tracebacks to the model (they consume tokens and confuse reasoning).
— not raw exceptions — from tool dispatch. The model can read
and decide to try a different tool or report the failure to the user. A raw Python traceback gives the model nothing useful and wastes context tokens.

Write tools that cause side effects — sending emails, inserting rows, deleting files — must never be called without the appropriate authorization. Two patterns guard write tools from accidental or malicious invocation:
: before executing a write tool, ask the human to confirm. The model calls a
tool; the application pauses and prompts the user; only on approval does the write proceed.
, the tool validates inputs and returns what
happen, without executing. This lets the model (and the developer) verify intent before committing.
Tool Definitions as a Schema Contract
The JSON Schema in a tool definition is a

between the model and the application. The model commits to producing arguments that match the schema; the application commits to accepting those arguments. Mismatches in either direction cause failures.
Model sometimes omits it; tool crashes
Add to required array; set a default in the handler
Model passes invalid values; handler must validate
Use enums, number types, and format hints
Model guesses what to pass; produces wrong values
Add a description to every property

text.text: No description on the tool itself

content.text: Model uses the tool in wrong contexts
Describe when to use AND when not to use
Model struggles to construct deeply nested inputs
Flatten the schema; accept top-level primitives

Treat tool schema authoring the same way you would treat API design. The schema is the interface — it determines correctness. A weak schema forces error handling everywhere downstream. A strong schema keeps the agentic loop clean.

The model decides what to do; tools do the doing. Design tools with the same care as a public API: clear names, minimal required arguments, explicit descriptions of when to use them, and structured return values. A well-designed tool set keeps the model accurate and reduces the need for error-handling in the agentic loop.
